coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-blended mixed amine for efficient CO2 capture under highly oxidative flue gas conditions
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Greenhouse Gas Control
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Sahoo, Prakash C․
Singh, Ravindra
Sivagurunathan, Periyasamy
Singh, Dheer
Kumar, Manoj
Gupta, R․P
Srivastava, Umish
coredata.dc:description: In the pursuit of developing efficient aqueous solutions for reversible CO2 capture in post-combustion carbon capture processes, the blending of amine solvents with advanced properties has emerged as a strategic approach. Regardless of several advantages of this method, challenges persist, particularly under high oxidative feed conditions, including amine component degradation, foaming, and low rich and lean loading. In this study, we address these inherent issues by investigating the potential of carbon dots (CDs) as additives to augment the performance of mixed amines consisting of Methyl diethanolamine (MDEA), 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) and Piperazine. Pilot scale operations at a 30 L solvent scale revealed that the CDs-additized mixed amine formulation exhibited robust performance over a continuous 200 h operation. The CDs-additized solvent exhibited a 23% higher rich loading than the non-additized counterpart, primarily due to enhanced mass transfer rather than the change in equilibrium loading. Additionally, the CDs-additized mixed amine solvent exhibited reduced foaming tendencies and approximately 16% lower regeneration energy consumption, even after prolonged continuous operation under oxidative flue gas condition. To evaluate the component degradation of the mixed amine solvent, a rapid and accurate Ionic chromatography (IC)-based method was developed. The present findings suggest that the CDs-additized mixed amine system holds promise as an efficient solvent for low-cost CO2 capture processes, ultimately contributing to the reduction of operational expenses in the CO2 capture process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CO2 capture
Amine degradation
Mixed amine
Ion chromatography
Method development

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM analysis of (a) and (b) water-stable carbon quantum dots, (c) and (d) amine-stable carbon quantum dots, and (e) amine-stable carbon dots under visible light and UV light.
Influence of CDs content on CO2 loading in mixed amine solution.
(a) Inlet concentration and percent CO2 capture in the absorber, (b) with mixed amine solvent, (c) with CDs-additized mixed amine, corresponding to L/G ratio of 2.1 L/Nm³.
Effect of time on HSS formation.
Desorption efficiency at various temperature using mixed amine solvent and CDs-additized solvent.
Effect of foam height using mixed amine and CDs additized amine.
Possible mechanism of CO2 capture enhancement using CDs blended amine.
Process conditions for CO2 capture using mixed amine and CD-additized mixed amine solutions.

row.entry: CO₂ (10 vol%), O₂ (15 vol%), SOx (1000 ppm), and NOx (1000 ppm)
CO₂ (10 vol%), O₂ (15 vol%), SOx (1000 ppm), and NOx (1000 ppm)

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Pressure mentioned in the study are gauge pressure. This means the actual operating pressures are above 1 bar.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-blended mixed amine for efficient CO2 capture under highly oxidative flue gas conditions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Indian Oil Corporation Ltd. R&D Centre, Sector-13, Faridabad, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Oil Corporation Ltd. R&D Centre

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Indian Oil Corporation Ltd. R&D Centre, Sector-13, Faridabad, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Indian Oil Corporation, R&D Centre, Sector-13, Faridabad, Haryana, 121007, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the pursuit of developing efficient aqueous solutions for reversible CO2 capture in post-combustion carbon capture processes, the blending of amine solvents with advanced properties has emerged as a strategic approach. Regardless of several advantages of this method, challenges persist, particularly under high oxidative feed conditions, including amine component degradation, foaming, and low rich and lean loading. In this study, we address these inherent issues by investigating the potential of carbon dots (CDs) as additives to augment the performance of mixed amines consisting of Methyl diethanolamine (MDEA), 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) and Piperazine. Pilot scale operations at a 30 L solvent scale revealed that the CDs-additized mixed amine formulation exhibited robust performance over a continuous 200 h operation. The CDs-additized solvent exhibited a 23% higher rich loading than the non-additized counterpart, primarily due to enhanced mass transfer rather than the change in equilibrium loading. Additionally, the CDs-additized mixed amine solvent exhibited reduced foaming tendencies and approximately 16% lower regeneration energy consumption, even after prolonged continuous operation under oxidative flue gas condition. To evaluate the component degradation of the mixed amine solvent, a rapid and accurate Ionic chromatography (IC)-based method was developed. The present findings suggest that the CDs-additized mixed amine system holds promise as an efficient solvent for low-cost CO2 capture processes, ultimately contributing to the reduction of operational expenses in the CO2 capture process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The imperative to address escalating environmental concerns has propelled the development of innovative strategies for efficient carbon dioxide (CO2) capture, particularly in post-combustion scenarios (El Hadri et al., 2017; Zanco et al., 2021). One such approach involves the blending of amine solvents with advanced properties, presenting a novel strategy for the formulation of effective solvent. Mixed amine solvents play a crucial role in carbon capture processes due to their unique advantages and improved performance compared to single amine systems (Wang et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2016; Idem et al., 2006; Luo et al., 2016). Their ability to address inherent limitations associated with individual amine solvents, coupled with the synergistic effect of combining different amines in a mixed solvent formulation, enhances the overall efficiency of CO2 absorption and desorption. This approach offers higher capture capacities, improved selectivity for CO2 over other gaseous components, and increased resistance to degradation under harsh operating conditions. The flexibility of mixed amine solvents allows tailoring of the solvent composition to specific industrial applications, optimizing performance for diverse flue gas compositions.

Several researchers have investigated the absorption rate of CO2 into mixed amines, including MEA-MDEA, MEA-TEA, AMP-MEA (Horng and Li, 2002; Sema et al., 2012; Choi et al., 2009). The synergy achieved in mixed amine systems, combining the higher equilibrium capacity of the tertiary amine (MDEA) with the elevated reaction rates of the primary (MEA) and secondary amine (DEA), significantly enhances the gas absorption rate. Studies have also explored the augmentation of reaction rates by incorporating piperazine into a mixture of potassium carbonate, MEA, tertiary amine, and steric hindrance amine (Tan and Chen, 2006; Li et al., 2023).

Despite their advantages, the implementation of mixed amine solvents in CO2 capture processes introduces challenges such as solvent high desorption energy, degradation, foaming, and compromised rich and lean loading, particularly under high oxidative feed conditions (Zhang et al., 2017; Ng et al., 2023; Mazari et al., 2014; Wang and Jens, 2014; Goff, 2005). Moreover, under high oxidative condition such as O2 higher than 15 mol%, the solvent faces major issues like high degradation and high foaming (Goff and Rochelle, 2005; Zhang et al., 2018). Addressing these challenges is imperative for advancing the viability of mixed amine systems in large-scale applications.

One effective strategy to address these challenges is the use of catalysts along with amines (Zhang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019, Li et al., 2023). The addition of catalysts to amine-based systems for CO2 capture plays an important role in advancing the efficacy of the capture process. Catalysts, typically nano catalysts accelerated the reaction kinetics, facilitating faster absorption of CO2 by the amines (Waseem et al., 2023; Bhatti et al., 2018). This enhancement in reaction rates translates to higher capture efficiencies, crucial for optimizing the performance of the capture system under variable conditions. Nano-catalysts such as SiO2, Al2O3, Fe2O3 and TiO2 have been reported to increased CO2 uptake capacity, and lowering regeneration energy (Zhang et al., 2023). Compared to pure solvents, nano-catalysed solvent showed faster CO2 absorption kinetics due to improved mass transfer (Devakki and Thomas, 2020).

However, most nano-catalysts face challenges, including formation of heat stable salt (HSS) during long-term operation and instability in amine solution, leading to blocking of packing materials. Additionally, under oxidative conditions, metallic nanoparticles accelerate the formation of free radicals, causing faster solvent degradation. Moreover, most studies are conducted on a very small scale, and no long-term studies have been carried out.

Carbon dot (CDs), due to their ultra-low size and multifaceted catalytic properties, present a promising alternative. In addition to their well-established photo-catalytic process, CDs showcase diverse catalytic properties that extend their applicability across various domains: electrocatalysts, fuel cells, pollutant degradation, water treatment, photothermal and organic catalysis (Guan et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2022). The electronic and chemical properties of CDs can be tailored by adjusting their size, shape, surface functional groups. CDs have gained considerable interest in energy conversion and storage applications due to their unique characteristics (Lim et al., 2015). The abundant functional groups (-OH, -COOH, -NH2, etc.) on CDs can act as active sites to further enhance catalytic performance through improved electron transfer.

Despite these advantages, the application of CDs in CO2 capture has not been extensively explored. Most research has focused on metallic nanoparticles. For example, Elhambakhsh et.al investigated the dispersion of six distinct magnetic nanoparticles in an aqueous N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone solution to enhance CO2 absorption capacity (Elhambakhsh and Keshavarz, 2020). Several studies have also assessed CO2 solubility in nanofluids in sealed containers. Rahimi et al. examined the influence of the host fluid and hydrophilicity of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) on the CO2 absorption capacity of an amine-based nano fluid (Rahimi et al., 2020).

This study indicates the effectiveness of CDs in improving mixed amine-based systems for CO2 capture. CDs blended mixed amine demonstrated enhanced CO2 capture efficiency and lower regeneration energy compared to neat mixed solvents. Furthermore, the CDs blended mixed amine system showed low foaming potential during prolonged application in a pilot plant at a 30 L scale under highly oxidative flue gas conditions. Further, assessing the individual amine components simultaneously in mixed amine is crucial for maintaining smooth operation of in CO2 capture processes as it helps to predict the solvent makeup rate. To assess mixed amine concentration rapidly and accurately, an ionic chromatography-based method was developed specifically for studying the CDs blended mixed amine. This study presents valuable insights into the potential of CDs to enhance the performance of mixed amine solvents in CO2 capture processes and addresses the perineal issues of conventional amine solvents.

All reagents used were of analytical grade and are used without further purification. Synthetic gas cylinders comprising CO₂ (10 vol%), O₂ (15 vol%), SOx (1000 ppm), and NOx (1000 ppm) were sourced from Chemicot Gas, New Delhi. Methyl diethanolamine (MDEA), 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP), citric acid cetyltrimethylammonium bromide, and piperazine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethylenediamine was also procured from Loba Chemie, India. Deionized water was utilized for the preparation of all aqueous solutions.

Carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method as described elsewhere (Wang et al., 2017). Typically, a mixture of citric acid (1 g) and ethylenediamine (2 g) was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The resulting solution was transferred to a microwave reactor for controlled irradiation. The microwave irradiation was conducted at 300 W for 2.5 min. The resulting carbon dots (CDs) were purified through centrifugation at 8000 rpm, followed by washing with ethanol and deionized water. The purified CDs were collected and stabilized with cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). The CDs were dispersed in water with sonication, and CTAB (800 mg/L) was added to the solution to ensure complete dissolution and stabilization of the CDs. CTAB stabilization is a process where CTAB (a cationic surfactant) is used to prevent aggregation and ensure the uniform dispersion of carbon dots (CDs) in solution. The stabilization mechanism involves both electrostatic and steric effects. The positively charged ammonium head group of CTAB bound to the negatively charged surface functional groups of the CDs. The hydrophobic tails of CTAB extend into the solution, forming a steric barrier that further inhibits particle interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-blended mixed amine solution
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the CDs-blended mixed amine solution, a solution containing 20 wt.% MDEA, 5 wt.% AMP, and 5 wt.% piperazine was prepared in the CTAB-stabilized CDs solution. This solution was stirred for 1 h, followed by sonication for 15 min. The final solution was placed in a shaker at 37 °C for 24 h. The prepared solution was subsequently used for the experimentation. The amount of CD added to the amine mixture is typically determined by the based on maximum CO₂ capture efficiency in the amine solvent. For the quantification of CDs, they were separated by centrifugation and subsequently dried overnight at 60 °C. The quantification of CDs was performed using gravimetric analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 capture and stripping using CDs-blended mixed amine
ce:section.ce:para: CO2 capture and stripping experiments were conducted in a pilot plant with a solvent carrying capacity of 30L. The pilot plant was fully automated with data recording capabilities. The absorber and stripper column had a height of 81 cm and a diameter of 3.2 cm, filled with inert alumina balls ranging from 2 to 3 mm in diameter as packing material. The plant included both an absorber and a stripper unit. The absorber was equipped with a thermostat and operated at content temperature. By maintaining a controlled and stable temperature in the absorber, the impact of CO₂ removal efficiency can be isolated and studied more effectively. The stripper was operated at various temperatures to achieve the desired CO2 stripping efficiency. Inlet and outlet gas samples were analysed using gas chromatography (GC) to determine CO2 concentrations. Lean and rich CO2 loadings were calculated using a titration method, detailed earlier (Masohan et al., 2009). Rich loading represents enhanced CO2 absorption kinetics due to improved mass transfer with CDs and may not correspond to equilibrium loading due to the lower absorber height. The absorber pressure was maintained at 0.5 bar, while the stripper pressure was set at 0.8 bar. The pressure values for the absorber and stripper presented in the manuscript are gauge pressures, which are measured relative to atmospheric pressure. Therefore, the reported pressures are above atmospheric pressure. Throughout the experiments, the liquid to gas (L/G) ratio was fixed at 2.1 L/NM3. The percentage of CO2 capture was calculated based on the CO2 concentrations measured in the inlet and outlet gas streams. Herein, the desorption efficiency is defined as the extent of CO₂ removed from the solvent during the desorption process. It is a measure of the solvent's ability to release absorbed CO₂ during regeneration. This efficiency is calculated using the following formula: Desorption Efficiency (%) = [(CO2 loading in rich solvent - CO2 loading in lean solvent)/ CO2 loading in rich solvent] × 100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of foaming tendency and heat stable salt (HSS) formation
ce:section.ce:para: The foaming tendency was assessed using a standard method described (Thitakamol et al., 2009). A foam vessel (inner diameter: 20.4 mm) was filled with 100 mL of solution and heated to 40 °C using a water bath, simulating the conditions in a post-combustion carbon capture plant. CO2 containing feed gas was bubbled through the solution at a flow rate of 10 mL/min at room temperature and 0.5 bar using a bubbler (diameter: 10 mm, pore size: 100–160 µm), controlled by a flow meter. After five minutes, the foam height was measured and recorded. The foam height experiment was conducted using a rich solvent with a CO₂ loading of 0.08 g CO₂/g solvent. In both cases, i.e., CDs-additized mixed amine solvent and neat mixed amine solvent, the same CO₂ loading was maintained. Fresh solvents were used for the foam tests. Heat stable salt (HSS) formation and individual amine concentration was analysed using ion chromatography (IC), as detailed in the supplementary information.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM 2100F, JEOL) was used to examine the morphology of the carbon dots (CDs). Heat stable salts (HSS) were analysed using ion chromatography. The HSS and residual amine content was analysed using a Dionex ion chromatograph (Model ICS 6000, Dionex, Sunnyvale, CA, USA) equipped with an ion conductivity detector in non-suppressed mode. For cation exchange, a Dionex IonPac CG17 (4 × 50 mm) guard column and a Dionex IonPac CS17 (4 × 250 mm) analytical column were used. The flow rate was set to 1.0 mL/min with an injection volume of 25.0 µL, achieving separation within 40 min. Data acquisition was performed using Chromeleon 7.2 software. Gas samples from the inlet and outlet were collected in Tedlar bags and analysed using gas chromatography (GC). Surface tension measurements were conducted at room temperature using a KRUSS Tensiometer (Model: K12) at a temperature of 40oC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characteristics of CDs blended mixed amine
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we have used CDs to improve mixed amine-based systems for CO2 capture. The CDs, when blended with mixed amine solutions, demonstrated enhanced CO2 capture efficiency and lower regeneration energy compared to neat mixed solvents. The density and viscosity of the unadditized solvent were measured at 1.07 g/cm³ and 2.1 cP, respectively. Notably, the addition of CDs did not alter the density and viscosity of the solution.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) analysis revealed that the as-synthesized carbon dots, with sizes ranging from 2 to 3 nm, exhibited significant agglomeration. However, when these carbon dots were dispersed in an amine solution, they became well-separated, as evidenced by the TEM images. The amine solution effectively dispersed the carbon dots, preventing agglomeration and resulting in a uniform distribution of individual particles. This dispersion can be attributed primarily to electrostatic repulsion, where the amines introduce charges on the surface of the carbon dots, creating a repulsive force that prevents agglomeration. The average particle size of CDs was analysed using particle size analyzer (Fig S2). It was found that CDs were uniformly distributed with mixed amine solvents and the mean size of the CDs were noted as 7.4 nm.

Under UV light illumination at 365 nm, the CDs sample emitted a bright blue fluorescence visible to the naked eye (Fig-1 e and f). Encouraged by the well-defined nano-suspension formation using CDs and mixed amine, we further evaluated the CO2 capture, stripping efficiency, and other important properties such as foaming tendency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CO2 capture experiment using CDs adultized mixed amine
ce:section.ce:para: Mixed amine systems have been found to be more effective in CO2 capture compared to single amine systems. Chen et al. demonstrated that various mixed amine systems exhibit excellent CO2 capture abilities and are more efficient than their single-component counterparts (Chen et al., 2021).

To assess the effectiveness of CD-additized solutions for CO2 capture, we conducted pilot plant experiments using a mixed amine solution composed of 20 wt.% MDEA, 5 wt.% 2-amino-AMP, and 5 wt.% piperazine, both with and without the addition of carbon dots (CDs). Both absorption and desorption experiments were carried out under identical conditions. The gas composition was maintained at CO₂ (10 vol%), O₂ (15 vol%), SOx (1000 ppm), and NOx (1000 ppm). Table-1 represents the process conditions for CO2 capture using both mixed amine solutions and CD-additized mixed amine solutions.

The CO2 loading of the nanofluids increased with the rise in CDs content from 50 mg/L to 100 mg/L. However, once the solid content of nanoparticles exceeded 100 mg/L, the enhancement factor began to decrease gradually (Fig. 2 ). As the CDs content increases, more nanoparticles populate the mass transfer film, boosting Brownian motion and enhancing the mass transfer rate. However, when the CDs content surpasses a certain threshold, the limited area of the gas–liquid interface, combined with increased particle agglomeration and precipitation, impedes mass transfer and results in lower absorption loading (Jiang et al., 2022).

Furthermore, surface tension of the mixed amine solution without CDs was 68.9 dynes/cm were also analysed. It was found that after adding CDs (100 mg/L), the surface tension decreased to 55.3 dynes/cm. CDs, may that interact with the solvent at the air-liquid interface, disrupting intermolecular forces and reducing surface tension. Lower surface tension is expected to enhance the mass transfer rate of CO₂ into the solvent by improving wettability and reducing the resistance at the gas-liquid interface (Pichetwanit et al., 2021).

A long-term experiment for 200 h was conducted, during which the absorption, desorption, and heat-stable salt (HSS) formation behaviors were analyzed in the pilot plant setup. As depicted in Fig. 3 , even after 200 h of absorption and stripping cycles, the absorption efficiency did not decrease. This observation highlights the stability and sustained performance of the CO2 capture process over an extended operational period. Interestingly, we observed that the absorption ability of the CD-unadditized amine solution gradually decreased over time. This decline is illustrated in Fig. 3, where it is evident that the CO2 capture efficiency decreases with prolonged operation. Moreover, Fig. 3 also demonstrates that the addition of CDs to the mixed amine solution resulted in approximately 16% higher CO2 capture efficiency compared to the neat mixed amine solution at the same liquid-to-gas (L/G) ratio. This enhancement underscores the effectiveness of CDs in improving CO2 capture performance and maintaining operational stability over extended periods of operation.

To investigate the cause of the decreased absorption capacity, the solvents were analyzed for heat-stable salt (HSS) formation and solvent concentration. Fig. 4 depicts the trend of HSS formation over time. As shown in the graph, HSS formation is higher in the CD-unadditized solvent compared to the CDs-additized solvent. Interestingly, under highly oxidative conditions like those of the feed gas used in this experiment, HSS formation was lower. This clearly indicates that the CDs-additized solvent exhibits an enhanced ability to handle higher oxidative feed gases.

The significantly higher stability of the CDs additized mixed amine solvent blend (MDEA, AMP, and PZ) towards degradation may be attributed to the unique antioxidative and radical-scavenging properties of the CDs. The degradation usually happens by the reaction of free radicals and reactive oxygen species with amines (Thompson et al., 2014). CDs may act as protective agents by intercepting reactive oxygen species and free radicals generated in the solvent during exposure to oxygen-rich feed gases, thereby reducing oxidative degradation pathways (Innocenzi and Stagi, 2023). CDs possess functional groups capable of neutralizing free radicals and reactive oxygen species. This prevents the chain reactions that lead to the oxidative degradation of amines.

The application of ion chromatography (IC) to organic amine ions is relatively uncommon, possibly due to the lower sensitivity of these species detectable using conductometric methods. In this study, we established a protocol for the simultaneous determination of alkanolamines, such as MDEA, AMP and piperazine by IC using a conductometric detection system in suppressed mode. This method enables baseline separation of these amines with excellent reproducibility, thereby enhancing the quantification and accuracy of the data.

Furthermore, we assessed the desorption efficiency of both CDs-additized and unadditized mixed amine solutions. As shown in Fig. 5 , at 115 °C, the desorption efficiency of the CDs-additized solvent is 18% higher than that of the unadditized solvent. This improvement can be attributed to the high thermal conductivity and surface area of CDs, which enhance heat transfer within the solvent system during desorption. Efficient heat transfer facilitates the rapid release of CO2 molecules from the solvent, thereby increasing desorption efficiency (Rozaiddin et al., 2022). The higher desorption efficiency observed with the CDs-additized solvent in Fig. 5 indicates that it requires less energy or effort to regenerate and reuse the solvent. This reduction in energy consumption is advantageous for operational cost savings and enhances the overall efficiency of the CO2 capture process.

Foaming tendency is a critical parameter that significantly impacts the operational efficiency of industrial plants, particularly those involved in CO2 capture processes. Effective control of foaming ensures smoother operations and prevents equipment downtime. In this study, we observed a reduction in foaming tendency when utilizing CDs-additized solvent, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . This finding underscores the substantial role of carbon dots (CDs) in mitigating foaming issues within solvent systems.

The observed decrease in foaming activity with CDs-additized solvent can be attributed to several mechanisms (Lee et al., 2016). Firstly, CDs may modify the surface tension of the solvent, thereby reducing the formation and stabilization of bubbles at the gas-liquid interface (Fig. 7 ). This modification discourages the persistence of foam. Additionally, the presence of CDs might enhance the stability of the solvent phase, inhibiting the entrapment of gas bubbles that contribute to foam formation. Moreover, CDs could potentially improve the wetting characteristics of the solvent on equipment surfaces, improve heat transfer, minimizing the nucleation sites for foam initiation.

This study demonstrates the promising potential of CDs as additives in mixed amine solvents for enhancing the efficiency of post-combustion CO2 capture processes. Through a comprehensive investigation involving a 30 L lab-scale operation for 200 h, the CDs-additized mixed amine formulation, comprising MDEA, AMP, and Piperazine, exhibited significant improvements in key performance metrics. Notably, the CDs-enhanced solvent achieved a 23% increase in rich CO2 loading and a 16% reduction in regeneration energy consumption compared to the non-additized solvent. Additionally, the incorporation of CDs mitigated common issues such as foaming and solvent degradation, thereby ensuring stable long-term operation under oxidative flue gas conditions. The study also introduced a rapid and accurate IC-based method to monitor the degradation of the solvent components, underscoring the practical applicability and efficiency of the CDs-additized mixed amine system. These findings highlight the potential of this innovative approach to lower operational costs and enhance the sustainability of CO2 capture processes. Moving forward, further research and optimization of CDs incorporation could pave the way for more widespread adoption and implementation in industrial-scale carbon capture applications.

Prakash C․ Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ravindra Singh: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Periyasamy Sivagurunathan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Dheer Singh: Visualization, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. Manoj Kumar: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. R․P Gupta: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. Umish Srivastava: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.ijggc.2025.104340.
